positives: It offers great performance for an air purifier under $200.
The filter has pelleted activated carbon, which is better than carbon-impregnated fabric.
It comes with auto mode and app support with the VeSync app.
You can remove the pre-filter for easy cleaning.
It doesn’t use an ionizer, so it is ozone-free.
It is relatively quiet — it hits 57.7 dBA at its top speed, and its performance at speed 2 (41.9 dBA) is very good.
The VeSync app unlocks smart modes and routines.
negatives: It only has one fan speed below 45 decibels (speed 1) and its performance at this speed is weak (170 m³/h).
There is a big jump in how loud this thing gets when you go from speed 1 (37.6 dBA) to speed 2 (49.4 dBA).
It comes with an optical sensor rather than a laser sensor, so you can’t forget to clean it regularly.

positives: It offers the best performance for $ spent across the 130+ air purifiers we tested.
It is the only high-CADR air purifier you can fit in a backpack.
The use of PC fans makes it quieter and more energy-efficient than most traditional retail air purifiers.
It comes with an AC-to-DC power adapter that includes a dial to adjust the voltage and sound levels.
You can choose between HEPA-only or HEPA with activated carbon; if you need to tackle odors or VOCs, get the filters with carbon.
negatives: The design is not for everyone, with a subscriber describing it to me as “a sci-fi toaster.”
It has no smart features, no air quality sensor, and no app support.
It’s in high demand, so it is often out of stock on Amazon; if you can’t find it there, check the official AirFanta shop.
You will have to build it yourself, but it takes just a couple of minutes to do so.

positives: It offers excellent air cleaning power even under the 45 dBA limit set for quiet performance tests (284 cfm).
You can use any brand of 20×25 MERV 13 filters, which leads to lower running costs.
It’s lightweight and comes with a handle for easy movement from room to room.
The body is made of lightweight wood, making it more sustainable than traditional plastic air cleaners.
You can buy the DIY kit, which is cheaper and includes everything you need to build it at home (except the filters).
negatives: The lightweight wood looks great, but the body is more delicate than plastic or metal you’ll find in other air purifiers.
It doesn’t come with carbon, so it won’t help with odors and chemical gases.
It’s a large device that won’t fit in smaller spaces; if you like the look but need a smaller footprint, you can choose the 5-fan version.

positives: It offers better air cleaning performance at top speed than other small models.
It has low running costs thanks to affordable filter replacements and low electricity consumption.
It comes with an onboard particle sensor that is highly responsive and unlocks auto-mode.
negatives: Speed 2 is just over the 45 dBA quiet performance limit, reaching 46.5 dBA.
The carbon-impregnated layer on the filter won’t make a significant difference against odors and gases.
It produces a sort of whistling sound when running at its lowest fan speeds, which may bother some users.

positives: It offers excellent performance at top speed, outmatching large units like the Winix 9800 and the Xiaomi Elite.
You can use it with any smart plug, so you can connect it to your smart home system or set simple automations.
The filter replacements are fairly priced and can last up to 12 months.
It looks like a million bucks — most people will appreciate the premium design.
negatives: It only has one fan speed below 45 dBA (speed 1) and its performance at this speed is weak (170 m³/h).
There is a big jump in noise when going from speed 1 (37.6 dBA) to speed 2 (49.4 dBA).
It uses an optical sensor rather than a laser sensor, so it needs frequent cleaning to stay accurate.

positives: It’s the fastest residential air purifier tested, ranking 2nd after the Smart Air Blast — estimated PM1 CADR of 462 cfm.
It comes with an onboard air quality sensor and shows real-time particle readings on screen paired with a color-coded LED light.
It features four smart modes (eco, silent, turbo, auto) for flexible power, noise, and energy consumption control.
It is made of high-quality materials and has a premium look and feel.
negatives: It is not cheap, with a list price just over $800.
Occasionally, the side covers can open accidentally when using the handles to move the unit.
It doesn’t come with app connectivity, which some buyers expect at this price point.

positives: It comes with 15 lbs of activated carbon with zeolite, the largest amount seen in a household air purifier.
Filters can last up to five years before needing replacement.
It has a one-dial operation that is easy to use right out of the box.
Its metal body is durable and comes mounted on high-quality castor wheels.
negatives: It has a high initial cost of just over $750 and comes with no smart features, sensor, or app support.
The large amount of carbon heavily restricts airflow, giving it the same CADR as the much smaller Levoit Core 300.
At 61.5 dBA, it gets quite loud at top speed.
At maximum fan speed, it pulls 148 watts, which is high compared to similar units.

positives: It doubles as a side table and fits most living rooms and bedrooms — you can even buy legs to give it more height.
It matches the air cleaning performance of high-end units that cost twice its price and are twice its size.
It comes with a laser sensor onboard that is highly accurate and doesn’t need regular cleaning.
The Blueair app has a clean UX and is easy to use.
You can customize the design with multiple pre-filter fabric colors and two leg design options.
negatives: It is more expensive to buy and run than other table air purifiers from IKEA and SwitchBot.
There is no option to disable the electrostatic ionization function, unlike competitors such as Winix, Coway, and Xiaomi.
The wooden legs and chrome base to turn it into a table are not included, adding up to $100 extra.

positives: It is super quiet — sound levels measured from 35.2 dB to 40.5 dB from lowest to highest fan speed.
It is compatible with generic PUREBURG filters, reducing yearly running costs if you prefer not to use CleanAirKits’ OEM filters.
It has an industrial feel with a thin wooden base and top that houses three cylindrical HEPA filters with activated carbon.
negatives: Retailing at $359, it is the most expensive of all the portable air purifiers tested, but it’s the only one that will actually clean your air and keep it clean on the go.

positives: It can clean a lot of air but only makes 54.7 dB(A) of sound at its top fan speed.
Very fair prices for new filters that provide 13 months of usage.
With an outer metal case instead of plastic, this unit is robust and built to last.
It has only a one-dial command to navigate through three fan speeds.
It’s a set-and-forget unit without any apps or fancy modes, and you can use it with a smart plug.
negatives: It comes at a high initial price of just over $1,000.
It is an oversized unit that takes up a lot of space, making it likely overkill for most homes.
It’s a heavy unit (38 kg), not designed to be moved easily despite having wheels.
It consumes significantly more energy than other large air purifiers, pulling 135.9 watts at top speed.

positives: It is an air purifier you can build yourself with parts you might already have available at home.
Excellent air cleaning performance for the price — one of the best ratios of performance to money spent.
You’ll be choosing the materials, so it’s super customizable and a fun project on its own.
All parts are readily available on Amazon.
negatives: It is a huge device and not the best looking — it takes up a lot of space.
It can run loudly and consume a lot of electricity, depending on the box fan you use.
There are no extra features you’ll find on commercial devices, like a child lock or the ability to schedule routines.
You don’t get the option to add a carbon filter to tackle VOCs and odors.

positives: Particle filtration rate score of 8.6 out of 10, bedroom score of 8.2, and pets score of 8.5.

Multi-stage filtration system with a stainless steel mesh pre-filter, two ’Bio-Guard’ filters for mold, viruses, and bacteria, an ozone filter, and a HEPA/pellet-type activated carbon filter to reduce recurring costs.
All parts except the HEPA/activated carbon filter have a 20-year lifespan and only need washing every couple of months.
Built-in particle sensors allow automatic fan speed adjustment based on current air quality.
Wi-Fi connectivity via the NuWave Connect app enables remote operation, air quality monitoring, scheduling, and settings changes.
Quiet operation at lower fan speeds ideal for overnight use.
negatives: HEPA/activated carbon filter requires yearly replacement.
Can become somewhat loud at higher fan speeds.

positives: Particle filtration rate score of 8.8 out of 10, bedroom score of 7.8, and pets score of 8.4.
Substantial clean air delivery rate suitable for studio apartments or large open-concept living and dining areas.
Wi-Fi enabled to view air quality estimates, adjust fan speed, and receive filter replacement alerts remotely.
negatives: Fractionally louder than the NuWave OxyPure at its lowest fan speed, making it less unobtrusive for overnight operation.
Fairly tall unit footprint may not fit well in low-clearance spaces.

positives: Particle filtration rate score of 8.0 out of 10, bedroom score of 7.7, and pets score of 7.9.
Three-stage filtration system similar to the Levoit Core 600S, effective for small to medium rooms.
Equipped with a particle ionizer that clumps smaller airborne particles for easier cleanup, with option to turn off ionizer.
negatives: Less powerful motor makes it less suited for larger rooms.
No Wi-Fi connectivity prevents remote operation and live air quality monitoring.
Ionizer produces ozone as a byproduct, which can irritate lungs with prolonged exposure.

positives: Particle filtration rate score of 6.0 out of 10, bedroom score of 7.2, and pets score of 6.4.
Compact enough to fit on a dresser or shelf yet offers sufficient CADR for bedrooms and nurseries.
Wi-Fi enabled with VeSync app for remote operation, scheduling, and filter replacement notifications.
negatives: Lower air-cleaning rate and poorer performance sealing the smallest airborne particles compared to larger machines like the Coway Airmega 200M.
Lacks automatic fan speed adjustment.

positives: Particle filtration rate score of 9.5 out of 10, pets score of 7.7.
Very high clean air delivery rate sufficient for big open-concept living spaces.
negatives: Ugly, bulky, and very noisy, making it unsuitable for living rooms or bedrooms.
Lacks convenience features such as particle sensors, Wi-Fi connectivity, and an odor-absorbing carbon filter.
Lacks convenience and automation features like incremental fan speed adjustment, resulting in a louder and less pleasant overall user experience compared to the NuWave OxyPure.

positives: Runs quietly and has a relatively small footprint.
negatives: Clean air delivery rate falls short of the Levoit Core 600S.

positives: Wide air inlet prevents hair from clogging the intake, making it an excellent option for pet owners.
negatives: Lags slightly behind the Coway Airmega 200M in terms of CADR.

positives: 10-year warranty.
Quiet.
Has app control.
negatives: No carbon filter to remove gases.

positives: Quiet.
Has a control-panel lock.
Wheels and handle make it easy to move around.
negatives: No app control or remote.
Air quality indicator lights are confusing.
Warranty is short for the price.

positives: Very effective for its price and size.
Back handle makes it easy to move around.
Three filters.
Ionizer to neutralize airborne particles.
negatives: Air quality indicator light is confusing.
Needs to be 8 inches from a wall or furniture.
Glossy finish attracts pet hair.
No app or remote control.

records.raw_product_name: O2+ Horizon 5-Stage Air Purifier with HEPA
positives: Slim, understated design.
Filters punch above their weight.
Great value.
negatives: No internal air sensor.
No app.
No auto mode.
Very loud at the highest setting.

positives: Powerful HEPA 14 filtration.
Negative oxygen ion technology.
Very quiet.
Portable.
negatives: Confusing display.
Negative oxygen ion technology in general can be dicey.

positives: Electrostatic filter for extended filter life up to five years.
Connects to the MyDyson App.
Auto setting.
negatives: Loud at highest setting.
Not yet CARB certified.
Filter isn't HEPA.

positives: Solid air cleaner at an inexpensive price.
negatives: No internal air sensor.
No app.
No auto mode.

positives: Surprisingly quiet.
Excellent app dashboard.
negatives: Awkward wheels for moving it around.

records.raw_product_name: Mini Restful Air Purifier Sunrise Clock
records.recommendation_type: Best Air Purifier That's Also a Sunrise Alarm Clock
positives: Doubles as a sunrise alarm clock.
Attractive, modern look.
negatives: Too loud for sleep at its highest setting.
No auto mode.
No air quality sensor.

positives: Handle for easier moving.
Environmentally friendly.
Indicator light rings on the control panel that show both indoor and outdoor air.
Long warranty.
negatives: Indicator lights are hard to see on the flat top surface.
Steep price.

positives: Quiet for a tower model.
Casters make it easy to move around.
Connects to the AirDoctor app.
negatives: One-year warranty is scant for the price.
Shipping box is unwieldy.

records.recommendation_type: Best Air Purifier for Wildfire Smoke
positives: Sleek looks.
Five-year warranty.
Has a remote.
Charcoal filter removes odors and VOCs.
negatives: Doesn't work with the Rabbit Air app.
Doesn't work with smart-home platforms.

records.recommendation_type: Best Air Purifier for Those Who Also Need a Heater
positives: Has a fan and a heater.
Easy-to-use app.
Indicator lights match the US air quality index.
negatives: Panel doesn't consistently illuminate when pressed.
Louder than expected.

positives: 10-year warranty.
Intuitive and easy to use.
Also captures toxic vapors and gases.
Effective for large square footage.
negatives: Needs to be 1 foot away from a wall.
Loud on high settings.

positives: Quiet.
Fun customizable panels.
Easy to use.
negatives: Pricier than similar purifiers.
Mood lighting is confusing.
Control panel turns off when not in use, creating an extra step.

positives: Pull-out prefilter collects pet hair.
H13 HEPA and carbon activated filter capture VOCs and odors.
Pet Mode quiets the fan when a pet is on the purifier.
negatives: Sits at ground level, which may be less responsive to pollutants higher in a room.
Lacks built-in heated pet bed that pet owners might expect.

positives: The filter scraped 81.5% of small particles (0.5 to 2.5 microns) in just 10 minutes
Removed 99% of all small and large particles from the medium-sized testing room in 27.2 minutes
Operating noise measured at 47.9 decibels on the highest setting, quieter than most home refrigerators
Estimated annual operating cost was $64.31 even when run continuously
Includes app control for monitoring indoor air quality and adjusting settings easily
negatives: Bulky design with no handles makes it difficult to move between rooms
A bit pricey, with a sticker price higher than many other purifiers tested

positives: Cleared smoky air in 46 minutes during testing
Estimated annual operating cost was $96.33
Interface can be locked to prevent accidental setting changes
negatives: Noise measured at 43.1 decibels, 6.3 decibels louder than the top pick
Not the most energy efficient compared to competitors

records.recommendation_type: Best Value Air Purifier for Small Spaces
positives: Average noise level of 36.5 decibels on highest setting and power consumption of 6.6 watts
Estimated annual operating cost was $30.36
Filter lasts 12 months and is inexpensive to replace
negatives: No advanced features beyond basic low, medium, high, and off settings
Air cleaning performance score of 4.8 may be insufficient for larger rooms

positives: Filter only needs to be changed every five years

Effectively masked odors, improving odor reduction

negatives: Took 76 minutes to clear 99% of small and large particulate matter
